skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs with Claude Code or Codex.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for building MCP servers that enable LLMs to interact with external APIs through well-designed tools.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation and guide resource for building MCP servers. The static scanner detected patterns (network calls, environment variable access, shell commands) in reference documentation files containing code examples. These are all false positives - the skill contains no executable code, only markdown documentation with example code snippets demonstrating standard API integration patterns.

content.user_title: Build MCP Servers for Claude Code
content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive guidance for building Model Context Protocol servers that enable Claude Code and Codex to interact with external APIs through well-designed tools. It covers TypeScript and Python implementations, best practices, and evaluation strategies.
actual_capabilities: Provides step-by-step guidance for building MCP servers from scratch
Includes TypeScript and Python implementation patterns and examples
Covers MCP protocol specification, transport mechanisms, and tool definitions
Offers best practices for tool naming, input validation, and error handling
Includes evaluation frameworks for testing MCP server quality
Documents environment variable configuration for API authentication
limitations: This is a documentation skill, not executable code - it provides guidance, not functionality
Does not include actual API integrations - users must implement their own
Requires users to have programming knowledge to follow the guides
Does not provide pre-built MCP servers for specific services

use_cases.title: Build a new MCP server for a custom API
use_cases.description: A developer wants to create an MCP server to integrate their company's internal API with Claude Code. They follow the step-by-step guide to plan, implement, and test their MCP server.

use_cases.title: Learn MCP protocol best practices
use_cases.description: A technical writer or product manager wants to understand how MCP servers work and what makes them effective for LLM integration.

use_cases.title: Evaluate existing MCP server quality
use_cases.description: An AI engineer wants to evaluate their MCP server implementation to ensure LLMs can effectively use the tools provided.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic MCP server
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a basic MCP server using the TypeScript SDK. I want to expose a simple API (like a todo list) with create, read, update, and delete tools. Show me the project structure and core implementation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new MCP server project

prompt_templates.title: Add authentication to MCP server
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I add API key authentication to my MCP server? I need to read the API key from an environment variable and include it in requests to the external service.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding authentication to an existing MCP server

prompt_templates.title: Improve tool descriptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my MCP server tool descriptions and suggest improvements. I want to make sure Claude Code can understand what each tool does and use them effectively. Here are my current tool definitions: [add tool definitions]
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing tool descriptions for LLM understanding

prompt_templates.title: Create evaluation tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create an evaluation framework for my MCP server. I want to test whether Claude Code can effectively use my tools to answer realistic questions about my API.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building quality assurance for MCP server

output_examples.input: How do I set up a basic MCP server project in TypeScript?
output_examples.output:
To set up a basic MCP server project in TypeScript:

1. Initialize a new Node.js project: npm init -y
2. Install the MCP TypeScript SDK: npm install @modelcontextprotocol/server-sdk
3. Create your server file with tool definitions using Zod for input validation
4. Register tools with the server and define the transport (stdio or Streamable HTTP)
5. Export the server for use with your chosen transport

The skill provides complete examples for each step with code snippets.

output_examples.input: What are the best practices for tool naming in MCP servers?
output_examples.output:
Best practices for MCP tool naming:

- Use consistent prefixes (e.g., github_create_issue, github_list_repos)
- Use action-oriented names that describe what the tool does
- Make names descriptive of the service being integrated
- Avoid version numbers in names
- Use clear, unambiguous names that help agents find the right tool quickly

output_examples.input: How should I structure error messages in my MCP server?
output_examples.output:
Error messages should be actionable and guide agents toward solutions:

- Include specific suggestions for fixing the error
- Provide next steps the agent can take
- Use standard JSON-RPC error codes
- Make error messages descriptive enough for the LLM to understand what went wrong and how to recover

best_practices: Prioritize comprehensive API coverage over high-level workflow tools to give agents flexibility
Use explicit tool definitions with title, description, inputSchema, and annotations
Design input validation with Zod (TypeScript) or Pydantic (Python) for runtime type safety
Test your MCP server with evaluation frameworks to ensure LLMs can effectively use your tools
anti_patterns: Avoid vague tool names that do not clearly describe the action performed
Do not skip input validation - always validate user inputs with proper schemas
Avoid returning overly verbose or unfiltered data from tools - design focused responses
Do not use generic error messages - provide actionable guidance for recovery

faq.question: What is MCP (Model Context Protocol)?
faq.answer: MCP is a protocol that enables LLMs like Claude Code to interact with external services through well-defined tools. It provides a standardized way for AI assistants to access APIs, databases, and other external resources.

faq.question: Which programming language should I use for my MCP server?
faq.answer: TypeScript is recommended due to high-quality SDK support and good compatibility with many execution environments. Python is also well-supported with the FastMCP framework. Both languages have comprehensive documentation in this skill.

faq.question: Do I need to implement all API endpoints for my service?
faq.answer: Prioritize comprehensive API coverage for core operations, but balance with specialized workflow tools for common tasks. When uncertain, err on the side of comprehensive coverage to give agents flexibility.

faq.question: How do I test if my MCP server works well with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Use the evaluation framework described in the skill to create test questions that LLMs must answer using only your MCP server tools. The quality of your server is measured by how well it enables LLMs to accomplish real-world tasks.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in my MCP server?
faq.answer: Read API keys from environment variables (process.env.YOUR_API_KEY) and include them in requests to external services. Never hardcode credentials. The skill provides examples for both TypeScript and Python implementations.

faq.question: What transport should I use for my MCP server?
faq.answer: Use stdio for local servers and Streamable HTTP for remote servers. Streamable HTTP uses stateless JSON which is simpler to scale and maintain compared to stateful sessions.
